items.correct: A small integrated chip containing CPU, memory, and I/O for controlling a device.
items.misconception: A very small computer like a laptop.
items.random_wrong: A remote control for a TV.
items.why: Students think microcontrollers are mini-PCs. They are specialised: limited resources, designed for a single embedded task. No operating system needed - just dedicated code running directly.

items.q: What does an LDR's resistance do as light intensity INCREASES?
items.why: Students assume 'more input = more resistance', but LDR resistance is INVERSELY proportional to light. Dark = high resistance; bright = low resistance. This is why LDRs detect darkness in circuits.

items.correct: Producing a specific quantity of identical items, then switching to make another batch.
items.why: Students confuse batch with mass production. Batch produces FIXED QUANTITIES with the line then changing - between one-off (single item) and mass (continuous high volume).

items.q: Whose 2005 Double Diamond models the design process?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: UK Design Council. “IDEO” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Which technique systematically alters product attributes?
items.why: The answer examiners want is: SCAMPER. “TRIZ” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: How many diamonds are in the Double Diamond model?
items.why: The real figure is 2 diamonds. The wrong options cluster deliberately close — anchor the exact number to one concrete fact so it can't drift.
